title: Which hosting model should Grill Visuals use for explicit sharing?
summary: Cloudflare Pages best matches immutable static artifacts and local-first generation; a Worker adds control but also storage, auth, and deletion complexity.

options.label: Use Cloudflare Pages
options.description: Deploys the existing inert artifact with immutable URLs and the least new operational surface.

options.label: Use Worker + KV
options.description: Best access control, but it creates a backend, storage contract, and provider coupling.

options.label: Use object storage
options.description: Portable and cheap, but aliases, cache invalidation, and verified deletion need more custom work.

options.label: Cloudflare Pages
options.summary: Deploy the self-contained site as an owned static project

options.label: Worker + KV
options.summary: Store session JSON and render through a custom edge application

options.label: Object storage
options.summary: Upload immutable HTML behind a public bucket or CDN

criteria.label: Privacy control
criteria.description: How narrowly can public exposure and deletion be managed?

criteria.label: Operational simplicity
criteria.description: How much new infrastructure must Grill Me own?

criteria.label: Immutable versions
criteria.description: Can every shared revision retain an exact artifact URL?

criteria.label: Verified unshare
criteria.description: Can the stable URL stop exposing session content predictably?

criteria.label: Cost
criteria.description: How cheaply does low-volume teammate sharing operate?

criteria.label: Portability
criteria.description: How coupled is the artifact to a proprietary runtime?

ratings.rationale: Explicit projects and deployments are recordable, but URLs are public once shared.
The existing inert site deploys without a custom backend.
Each deployment receives an immutable deployment URL.
One owned project per live share lets unshare delete the exact project and verify that the session marker is gone.
Static low-volume artifacts fit comfortably within platform limits.
The artifact is plain HTML even though deployment automation targets Cloudflare.
A Worker can enforce tokens, expiry, and request policy.
It introduces an application runtime, storage model, migrations, and operational ownership.
Immutable versions need a custom key and retention contract.
Deleting or denying a storage key can take effect immediately.
Runtime and storage are inexpensive but no longer zero-maintenance.
Rendering and access policy become tied to Worker and KV behavior.
Bucket policy is powerful but easy to misconfigure for anonymous links.
Upload is simple; safe public routing and cleanup still need conventions.
Content-addressed object keys naturally preserve exact versions.
CDN caches and aliases can outlive object deletion without careful invalidation.
Small static artifacts are extremely cheap to store and serve.
The object and URL model maps cleanly across providers.
